question: Will Newcastle United Football Club renege on their club badge redesign before August 16, 2025?
resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, before August 16, 2025, Newcastle United Football Club officially and explicitly announce that the club badge will not be redesigned. It will also resolve as **Yes** if a new design is announced and, before August 16, 2025, Newcastle officially announces that they will revert to the old design.
background:
Newcastle United, one of Britain's oldest and most popular football clubs, recently [announced](https://www.theguardian.com/football/2025/may/09/newcastle-consult-fans-over-plans-to-redesign-outdated-club-badge) their intention to redesign their club badge:

> Newcastle have risked upsetting some supporters by announcing plans to redesign the club’s badge for the 2026-27 season. With St James’ Park executives deciding the crest, that dates back to 1988, is overly elaborate and unsuited to global digital branding requirements, Newcastle are working with designers and consulting fans as they begin rebranding a symbol they believe has become outdated.

###### Guardian: [Newcastle consult fans over plans to redesign ‘outdated’ club badge](https://www.theguardian.com/football/2025/may/09/newcastle-consult-fans-over-plans-to-redesign-outdated-club-badge)

The club are not alone in their redesign campaign as badge redesigns to fit the modern era are becoming increasingly common. For example, [Juventus FC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Juventus_FC), one of the oldest and most decorated football clubs in the world, [announced](https://web.archive.org/web/20170118033701/http://www.juventus.com/en/news/news/2017/black-and-white-and-more-juventus-future-now-.php) a major logo redesign in 2017, ditching their traditional club badge for an ultra-modern design.

These modern proposals, along with the accompanying corporate jargon used to justify them, often stand in stark contrast to the traditions that many football fans hold dear. For example, in 2018, Leeds United, one of Britain’s oldest and most popular football clubs, announced a major badge redesign, replacing their traditional emblem for one with a modern twist. However, [widespread backlash](https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/42803838) from supporters, fellow clubs, and the general public led the club to swiftly abandon the new design, despite months of research and development.
